metadata.title: The Instagram/Facebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery: A sentiment analysis and topic modeling approach

abstract: Research indicates that adolescents who are vulnerable to self-harm are likely to access social media specifically related to self-harm, which often contains health myths and fails to provide linkages to appropriate resources. In this study, we examined the emotional discourse before and following a ban of graphic images of self-harm on Facebook and Instagram. This observational case series study assesses a corpus of 8,013 tweets that specifically mention Facebook and/or Instagram between June 1, 2018, and May 31, 2019 (the ban occurred on February 7, 2019). Topical content was elucidated via statistical modeling to extract abstract topics in discourse. The graphic self-harm ban produced emotional shifts in users of social media. A time series plot produced peaks of tweets on the banning of graphic images of self-harm including political events, news coverage, and media exemplars of suicide (both celebrity and noncelebrity). We observed raw proportional differences with elevated emotions before the ban (i.e., disgust, joy, surprise, and trust) followed by elevated emotions occurring after the ban (i.e., anger, anticipation, and sadness). This study provides insight into how the policychange relating to self-harm was received by those with a vested interest. These insights can be valuable to policymakers and advocates for those affected by self-harm.

sections.paper_id: Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: On February 7th, 2019, Head of Instagram Adam Mosseri issued a public statement indicating that, following comprehensive reviews with experts and academicians, the social media platform would no longer allow the posting of graphic images of self-harm, such as cutting (Instagram, 2019). On the same day, Facebook, which acquired Instagram in 2012, also banned graphic images of self-harm due to the potential risk that such imagery could unintentionally promote the behavior (Facebook, 2019). This ban has remained in effect, although non-graphic imagery related to self-harm continues to be permissible, with the goal being the promotion of positive health messages and referring vulnerable and/or distressed people in need to available health care resources. Addressing the content on self-harm as posted to social media platforms became a critical issue following several events that generated significant news coverage. This included, but was not limited to, several livestreaming suicides on Facebook, the death of 14-year-old British teenager Molly Russell who viewed self-harming imagery on Instagram before her suicide, and an earlier Tumblr ban on blogs that advocated self-harm following the suicide of another British teenager, Tallulah Wilson (Jacobs, 2019;Tumblr Staff, 2012).

Self-harm, also known as non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI), is a behavior that disproportionately impacts adolescents. Self-harm is defined as the deliberate destruction or alteration of body tissue without conscious suicidal intent (Favazza, 1989;Nock & Favazza, 2009). Prevalence estimates for self-harm in adolescence range from 13.9% to 21.4%, with higher estimates found in adolescents who have experienced trauma from abuse, incarceration, or human trafficking (Kiss et al., 2015;McReynolds et al., 2017;Moreno et al., 2016;Nock & Favazza, 2009;Patchin & Hinduja, 2017;Wang et al., 2017). A meta-analysis of studies published between 1990 and 2015 on adolescent self-harm found that the mean age of starting self-harm was 13 years, with 47% reporting only one or two episodes, and cutting being the most common type (45%) (Gillies et al., 2018). This metaanalysis featured 172 datasets reporting self-harm in 597,548 participants from 41 countries (Gillies et al., 2018).

Self-harm during adolescence carries risk of depression/anxiety, delinquency/aggressive behavior, suicide, alcohol/drug use, poor school performance, dysfunctional interpersonal relationships, and physical scarring (Kiss et al., 2015;McReynolds et al., 2017;Nock & Favazza, 2009). Suicide shares many of the same risk factors as self-harm, with elevated risk of both behaviors occurring in 15-to 19-year-old youth (Hawton et al., 2012;Hilton, 2017). Suicide is the second leading cause of death for young people worldwide, with suicide attempts carrying increased risk of increased suicidal ideation and behaviors in the future (Arendt, 2018;Groholt & Ekeberg, 2009;Hawton et al., 2012). While self-harm does not typically include suicidal intent, the two behaviors are highly correlated in adolescent populations (Whitlock & Knox, 2007) representing a significant challenge for health care providers.

To date, most empirical research has focused on the relationship between social media and suicide ideation and/or behaviors, with little recognition of self-injurious behaviors. For example, a recent assessment of 30 adolescents who were hospitalized for a suicide attempt or severe ideation found social media usage to offer both positive and negative influences (Weinstein et al., 2021). Positive aspects included the themes of social connection, social support, affect-enhancing content, shared interests, and resources for mental health and coping. Negative social technology uses included trouble regulating use, stress related to social media metrics, encounters with "triggering" content, hostility and meanness, self-denigrating comparisons, and burdensome friendship expectations (Weinstein et al., 2021). Similar findings were evident in Nesi et al. ( 2019)'s investigation of psychiatrically hospitalized adolescents (aged 11-18), where youth in acute crisis who had previously attempted suicide reported mixed feelings towards social media. This included both positive social media uses (i.e., distraction, social support) and negative social media uses (i.e., getting into a fight or argument, making self-denigrating social comparisons, feeling left out, being bullied or teased, and viewing content that encouraged self-injury or suicide; Nesi et al., 2019).

Twitter has served as an effective social media platform for investigating self-harm. Jashinsky et al. (2014) assessed 1,659,274 tweets over a 3-month period and identified 37,717 tweets as at-risk for suicide. Higher suicidal risk was found in midwestern and western parts of the United States, with the researchers suggesting that Twitter may be a viable tool for real-time monitoring of suicide risk on a large scale (Jashinsky et al., 2014). Using a dataset of Twitter posts O' Dea et al. (2017) found that suicide-related posts had unique linguistic profiles, particularly the use of higher word counts, the increased use of first-person pronouns, and more references to death. This indicates that the analysis of Twitter data could help to validate online risk assessments and determine those in need of further support or intervention.

Empirical work regarding social media and self-injurious behavior is less common. Several investigations into Youtube content, specifically viewer comments on the 100 most viewed videos about self-injury, reveal that users may maintain the behavior (by sharing their own self-injury experiences) with few references towards positive message and/or recovery (Lewis et al., 2011(Lewis et al., , 2012)). Over 90% of videos featured non-suicidal self-injury photographs devoid of content warnings, leading the researchers to postulate that selfinjury was being normalized by this process (Lewis et al., 2011). Self-injury was also popular on Instagram, with postings following ambiguous hashtags (e.g., #blithe, #cat, and #selfinjuryy), a lack of content warnings, and few linkages to positive information and/or referrals to health care resources (Moreno et al., 2016). A review of 362 Twitter messages identified five key themes: celebrity influence, self-harm is not a joke (with subthemes of "you wouldn't laugh if you loved me" and "you think it's funny, I think it's cruel"), support for and from others, eating disorders and self-harm, and videos and personal stories (Hilton, 2017). Hilton (2017) also found that while Twitter provided a measure of support for those who self-harm, particularly by providing a sense of community, relatedness, and understanding, there was also risk that such support contributed to normalizing and possibly perpetuating self-injurious behavior. Online interactions also include risk from parallel issues like digital bullying that can increase acts of self-injury (Patchin & Hinduja, 2017).

Research indicates that adolescents who are vulnerable to self-harm are likely to access websites and social media dedicated to self-harm content (Lewis et al., 2011(Lewis et al., , 2012(Lewis et al., , 2014;;Wang et al., 2017). When compared to adolescents who do not selfharm, the adolescents who engage in self-harm are more likely to use the internet in general (Lewis et al., 2011(Lewis et al., , 2012)). Wang and colleagues found that self-harm users found it easier to discuss self-harm-related thoughts and behaviors on social media than in the physical world (Wang et al., 2017). Motivations for engaging in social media regarding selfharm are based on a need for validation, assurance, and support. However, online participation in self-harm forums carries risk of negative health outcomes. Social media websites often lack warnings about explicit content (Lewis et al., 2014;Moreno et al., 2016) and include no information regarding available health resources, professional interventions, or positive recovery-orientated content for adolescents who self-harm (Arendt, 2018;Lewis et al., 2012). The content on social media often includes health myths and misinformation about self-harm and produces feelings of hopelessness, sadness, selfhate, and loneliness (Arendt, 2018;Lewis et al., 2014). Information available online often normalizes self-harm and possibly perpetuates further acts of self-harm, suicidal ideation, and contagion effects (Arendt, 2018;Lewis et al., 2012Lewis et al., , 2014;;Patchin & Hinduja, 2017;Rodham et al., 2007;Seko et al., 2015).

Twitter represents an appropriate social media platform to investigate emotional discourse regarding the ban of graphic images of self-harm for several reasons. Twitter, unlike Facebook and Instagram, allows for searches of all public user posts according to specific words and phrases and thus has the capacity to generate a rich data corpus (Hilton, 2017;O'Dea et al., 2017). Moreover, Twitter has been documented as a viable tool for real-time monitoring of suicide risk (Jashinsky et al., 2014). Those at-risk for suicide post unique linguistic profiles that may offer utility for the development of online risk assessments (O'Dea et al., 2015(O'Dea et al., , 2017)). Further, Twitter is a platform based on shared interest (often anonymous, or at least deidentified) rather than social proximity. Since communication on Twitter often occurs between people who do not know each other in real life, those with prior experiences with self-harm, and thus strong feelings, may be more likely to share their thoughts compared to a nonymous network (e.g., Facebook). Those who work in the sphere of policy strategy may be more likely to use a network like Twitter to create movements and spur change.

The ban on graphic self-imagery by large social media platforms is a form of content moderation. Roberts (2017) argues that as social media platforms continue to grow in financial, political, and social domains, there is a concomitant need to control user expressions through content moderation. To scholars like York and Zuckerman (2019), "content moderation is a wicked problem for UGC (user-generated content) companies for which there are no easy solutions" (p. 139). While graphic images of self-injury have not been explored by these researchers, another self-harming behavior (i.e., eating disorders) has been analyzed, providing evidence that 69% of removed posts on Instagram were distinct from non-removed posts (Chancellor et al., 2016). Removed posts were more toxic, included more references to self-harm, and featured more vulnerable content (Chancellor et al., 2016). Clearly the intersection of social media use, mental health issues, and content moderation it is an important issue that requires empirical investigation (see Gerrard, 2020).

The current study is the first to utilize a large dataset of social media tweets within the context of a substantial policy change regarding graphic images of self-injury. While many have researched and developed algorithms for detecting posts about self-harm in general, this is one of the first studies to our knowledge that employs data mining techniques (i.e., sentiment analysis and topic modeling) to measure emotional content regarding self-harm as expressed on social media following a ban on graphic self-injury. Alhassan et al. (2021) have posted a paper on Arxiv that explores the sentiment and topic content of posts by support accounts on Twitter to propose techniques for mitigating potential actions of selfharm. Similarly, we use text analysis techniques to evaluate social media discourse occurring during a policy ban on graphic self-harm imagery by Facebook and Instagram. This study explores three research questions: (1) What drives conversations about self-harm policy on Twitter? (2) Did the sentiment of discourse about self-harm policy change after the ban on graphic self-harm imagery in tweets that mention "Facebook" or "Instagram"? (3) Did topic contents change after the ban on graphic self-harm imagery in tweets that mention "Facebook" or "Instagram"? That is, we offer insight into policy development by exploring the narratives used in discourse about self-harm and to evaluate the reactions after the decision to ban graphic images of self-harm (i.e., positive reactions to unintended consequences) among those who are active in discussions about self-harm on Twitter (e.g., self-harmers, and self-harm activists).

To scholars like York and Zuckerman (2019), "content moderation is a wicked problem for UGC (user-generated content) companies for which there are no easy solutions" (p. 139). While graphic images of self-injury have not been explored by these researchers, another self-harming behavior (i.e., eating disorders) has been analyzed, providing evidence that 69% of removed posts on Instagram were distinct from non-removed posts (Chancellor et al., 2016). Removed posts were more toxic, included more references to self-harm, and featured more vulnerable content (Chancellor et al., 2016). Clearly the intersection of social media use, mental health issues, and content moderation it is an important issue that requires empirical investigation (see Gerrard, 2020).

The current study is the first to utilize a large dataset of social media tweets within the context of a substantial policy change regarding graphic images of self-injury. While many have researched and developed algorithms for detecting posts about self-harm in general, this is one of the first studies to our knowledge that employs data mining techniques (i.e., sentiment analysis and topic modeling) to measure emotional content regarding self-harm as expressed on social media following a ban on graphic self-injury. Alhassan et al. (2021) have posted a paper on Arxiv that explores the sentiment and topic content of posts by support accounts on Twitter to propose techniques for mitigating potential actions of selfharm. Similarly, we use text analysis techniques to evaluate social media discourse occurring during a policy ban on graphic self-harm imagery by Facebook and Instagram. This study explores three research questions: (1) What drives conversations about self-harm policy on Twitter? (2) Did the sentiment of discourse about self-harm policy change after the ban on graphic self-harm imagery in tweets that mention "Facebook" or "Instagram"? (3) Did topic contents change after the ban on graphic self-harm imagery in tweets that mention "Facebook" or "Instagram"? That is, we offer insight into policy development by exploring the narratives used in discourse about self-harm and to evaluate the reactions after the decision to ban graphic images of self-harm (i.e., positive reactions to unintended consequences) among those who are active in discussions about self-harm on Twitter (e.g., self-harmers, and self-harm activists).
sections.preview_head: On February 7th, 2019, Head of Instagram Adam Mosseri issued a public statement indicating that, following comprehensive reviews with experts and academicians, the social media platform would no longer allow the posting of graphic images of self-harm, such as cutting (Instagram, 2019). On the same day, Facebook, which acquired Instagram in 2012, also banned graphic images of self-harm due to the potential risk that such imagery could unintentionally promote the behavior (Facebook, 2019). This ban has remained in effect, although non-graphic imagery related to self-harm continues to be permissible, with the goal being the promotion of positive health messages and referring vulnerable and/or distressed people in need to available health care resources.

Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: These data consist of 238,001 tweets, in English, excluding retweets and quotes, that contain "self-injurious behavior," "self-injurious behavior," "nonsuicidal self-injury," or "self-harm" from June 1, 2018, to May 31, 2019, obtained from the premium Twitter API (Twitter Dev, 2018). Of this corpus of tweets, there were 8,013 that specifically mention Facebook and/or Instagram. These 8,013 tweets are used for the sentiment analysis and topic modeling. Search terms were based on previous studies on adolescent self-harm and the internet (Arendt, 2018;Lewis et al., 2011;Moreno et al., 2016;Rodham et al., 2007;Wang et al., 2017).

Our data collection and analyses were reviewed by the Colgate University Institutional Review Board (IRB). It was determined that this project is exempt from the review process according to the Code of Federal Regulations Title 45, Part 46 (Exemption 2: Public data). While self-harm is an important and sensitive topic, this is an observational study, and we do not interact with Twitter users as vulnerable experimental subjects. In this study, we passively observe how Twitter users engage with the platform without intervention or experimental control. Further, while tweets are identifiable to the username level, we do not report any user-level data within. The analyses reported are aggregated and preserve anonymity beyond just the user-level-that is, we do not expose any tweets or Twitter users to a larger audience than they inherently have as public posts to the social media network.

paragraphs: A time series plot of the 238,001 tweets, created using the ggplot2 package (Wickham, 2016) for R (R Core Team, 2019), can be found in Figure 1. The time series plot F I G U R E 1 A time series plot of tweets about self-harm from June 1, 2018 to May 31, 2019. The true count of tweets is plotted in gray and a 3-day simple moving average is plotted in black indicates a prolonged peak of tweets about self-harm surrounding the announcement of the ban; however, other peak events can also be noted in the figure.

A description of the major events that led to heightened Twitter activity can be found in Table 1. This table indicates days with the highest number of tweets were associated with the Children's Survey of self-harm, followed by the reporting of the ban and the announcement of the ban. Tweets about the ban of graphic imagery focus on political events, like the Head of Instagram meeting with the UK Health Secretary and high rates of self-harm occurring in prison. Twitter activity also peaked around media exemplars of suicide (i.e., Molly Russell), self-harm events in popular shows (i.e., a Hollyoaks character dies from complications due to self-harm), and self-harm scares covered by the media (i.e., reporting of MOMO warning).

paragraphs: To compare word sentiment and emotional content in tweets before and after Instagram and Facebook's ban of graphic images of self-harm, February 07, 2019, the proportion of tweets containing at least one word that elicits each of the eight basic emotions was measured. This involved the use of the tidytext package (Silge & Robinson, 2016) coupled with the NRC lexicon (Mohammad & Turney, 2013) in the syuzhet package (Jockers, 2017) for R. The NRC Lexicon is a list of English words and their associations with eight basic emotionsanger, fear, anticipation, trust, surprise, sadness, joy, and disgust. For each tweet, we record the presence of words that elicit each emotion. This provides a baseline measure of the emotion content of tweets using a shared lexicon for labeling words. We chose to report the emotional content in this fashion instead of the raw count of terms because the fact that a user mentioned three terms that elicit negativity doesn't mean that their view is more negative than a user who only used one. The first user may be more verbose, be tweeting on a computer rather than a phone, and so forth. The emotive terms "ban," "harm," "injurious," "injury," "suicide," and "suicidal" were removed from the dataset for sentiment analysis after manual review of the sentiment measured in a large sample of tweets as they represented a tautology that unduly affected the measured sentiment given the topic.

sections.paper_id: Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: Topics about self-harm were programmatically modeled using latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003), a common algorithm for topic modeling, using the topicmodels package (Grün & Hornik, 2011) for R. This is an unsupervised classification technique which only requires a prespecified number of topics and the tweets themselves to elucidate topics in the tweets. LDA is a flexible probabilistic model for describing the probability of selecting a specific word when sampling from a particular topic. Given a corpus of tweets, LDA defines every topic as a mixture of words and every tweet as a mixture of topics. In the initialization phase, LDA defines topic representations by randomly assigning words to topics; that is, each topic is a distribution over words. These assignments are completed iteratively via Gibbs sampling with the goal of maximizing the probability that each topic generates the words assigned to it. Each tweet is then considered a mixture of topics, where each word of a tweet is viewed as being drawn from a particular topic. This methodology allows tweets to cover multiple topics in terms of self-harm content, rather than being separated into strictly disjoint topics in a way that mirrors a human reader's approach.

The following preprocessing steps were conducted for model fitting. The UDPipe package (Wijffels, 2019) in R provides a natural language processing toolkit that was used for tagging the parts of speech of words in each tweet. It has been shown that considering the parts of speech in topic models improves topic coherence (Feinerer et al., 2008;Maier et al., 2018;Witten & Frank, 2002). The LDA model is based on the proper nouns, nouns, and verbs of each tweet to create synthetic, simple sentences that retain their basic substance. The tm package (Feinerer & Hornik, 2019) in R provides various tools for transforming and cleaning text. This was used to transform the corpus of self-harm tweets by eliminating extra whitespace, extra spaces or empty lines; removing link URLs; removing punctuation; removing numbers; converting to lowercase, so that words are matched regardless of raw capitalization; and removing stop words, words that frequently occur in most English texts but provide little or no information and words that are in many tweets due to our search terms and other ubiquitous terms; that is, "self," "harm," "injurious," "injury," and "suicidal," "Instagram," "Facebook," "selfharm," and "graphic." The RWeka package (Witten & Frank, 2002) in R provides methods for creating n-gram tokens. For example, a bi-gram token is a two-word phrase like "Instagram ban." This yielded an LDA model fit on two-word phrases instead of single words. The motivation for using bi-grams from the synthetic, simple sentences is, in short, to capture nouns and verbs.

LDA models, further, require the selection of the number of topics; this is unknown a priori, and initial estimates were found using the approach of Cao et al. (2009) with subsequent human interpretation of topic model cohesion near the initial estimates (i.e., fewer topics and more topics). For the topic models fit to tweets mentioning "Facebook" or "Instagram," we found the number of topics that lead to optimal topic cohesion to be six for tweets before the ban and five for tweets after the ban. This manual check for validity included extensively searching and analyzing samples of tweets containing topic-specific bi-grams and assessing cohesion. We further explored the contents of the resulting topics externally as many topics surrounded specific aspects of the major events and news highlighted in Figure 1, which we examine further in the results section.

sections.paper_id: Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: There were statistically significant relationships between the proportion of tweets that contain at least one word that elicits anger, anticipation, disgust, joy, sadness, surprise, and trust and the timing of the tweet-before or after the ban on graphic images of self-harm on February 7th, 2019. There was no significant association between the proportion of tweets that contain at least one word that elicits fear and the timing of the tweet. The raw proportional differences suggest a decrease in disgust, joy, surprise, and trust and an increase in anger, anticipation, and sadness following the ban. While sentiments regarding Tweets include a wide range of content statements, we examined key themes in the elevated emotions before the ban (i.e., disgust, joy, surprise, and trust) and the elevated emotions that occurred following the ban (i.e., anger, anticipation, and sadness) as this follows the temporal time order of the ban. In what follows, we report common themes observed during a manual review of the sentiment analysis in several samples of tweets.

Disgust before the ban was also connected to pre-ban sentiments that photos of "living with scars" would soon be censored. (i.e., Jada Pinkett Smith) and the "excellent acting" involving lethal self-harm on the UK-based television show Hollyoaks. Joy and trust were expressed more before the ban, specifically tweets endorsing the movement to save lives and create new online tools to prevent self-harm. The presence of tweets involving trust before the ban centered on the impending ban validating that the problem of self-harm is real, with terms like "respect," "share," "friend," and "honest/blunt opinion" being prominent. Trust also revealed a narrative of "hope it save lives" expectation of the ban.

Increases in anger and sadness following the ban were associated with the continued presence of graphic self-harm imagery on Instagram and Facebook. This coincided with the awareness that self-harm clean celebrations were also being censored as part of the ban on images of graphic self-harm. Anger also included comments about the continued appearance of content on topics of self-harm, hate crimes, and child abuse that remained on both Instagram and Facebook. Increased sadness following the ban was related to postings about Molly Russell's suicide as her death was linked to the policy change. Increases in anticipation were based on the potential impact of the ban on other social media platforms and blogs. Tweets also increased in the anticipation that the ban would move from a focus on graphic imagery towards addressing the root causes of self-harm. Increases in sadness and surprise following the ban were linked to a May 2019 event in which a 16-year old Malaysian girl posted an Instagram poll asking followers if she should commit suicide or not-the girl died by suicide after 69% of responders voted that she should (Fullerton, 2019).

sections.paper_id: Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: Topics before (b) the February 7th, 2019 Instagram and Facebook ban on graphic self-harm imagery were largely centered on user expectations and political aspects of the new policy. This includes tweets in reaction to Head of Instagram Adam Mosseri meeting with a British health secretary over self-harm content (topic 5b; BBC News, 2019a) and an op-ed where Mosseri argued that social networks have not adequately protected users from self-harm imagery (topic 3b; The Telegraph, 2019). Tweets were also generated in response to Nick Clegg (Facebook VP) stating they will do it takes" to make its social media platforms safer (topic 4b; BBC News, 2019c), including the use of a Facebook Artificial Intelligence-driven detection system that predicts suicide risk and reports suicidal events to authorities (Kaste, 2018).

Before-the-ban topics were also centered on tweets regarding images of self-harm in general. Topics included a news story where a father discussed saving his daughter, a 12-year girl named Libby, from self-harm content on Instagram, which he described as more graphic than he observed while serving in the Army (topic 6b; BBC News, 2019b). In a separate topic, the story was shared through the daughter's lens-Libby tells BBC how she became "hooked" on posting and viewing self-harm images on Instagram, including pictures of cutting, burning, and overdosing, and how those images "made it safe to do it worse" (topic 1b; BBC News, 2019b). Tweets also referred to problems associated with moderating content about suicide and self-harm and the pre-ban use of sensitivity screens to hide images of self-harm (topic 2b; Birnbaum, 2019).

General topics that emerged after (a) the February 7th, 2019 ban were more focused on reactions to the changes in graphic imagery on Instagram and Facebook, media portrayals of self-harm, and individuals' exemplars of self-harm. Individual exemplars highlighted tweet responses about Molly Russell, a 14-year-old girl who committed suicide in 2017. Topics included her father's statement that graphic imagery on Instagram contributed to her death and a New York Times article that connected Molly Russell's death to her participation on Instagram. Tweets also focused on media representations, such as actress Jada Pinkett Smith sharing her personal struggle with self-harm.

These topics included reactions to a New York Times story about the ban on graphic images of self-harm as related to Molly Russell (topic 1a; Jacobs, 2019), as well as, media examples that appeared to encourage self-harm such as the MOMO challenge, Peppa Pig, Fortnite, etc (topic 2a; Lewis, 2019). The topics centered on changes to the policy featured tweets about the new rules designed to start the censoring images of self-harm and suicide and social media commitment to removing unacceptable images of self-harm-"taking action"-(topic 3a; Hatmaker, 2019). This included users concerned with the censoring of images-"photos heal"-(topic 5a; Bramwell, 2019) as well as users who discuss the continued prevalence of "unacceptable" images (topic 4a; Malik, 2019).

sections.paper_id: Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: Self-harm is an important issue to study as it is a prevalent behavior in adolescent samples, with adolescents likely to engage in social media to seek health information that can be misleading and potentially dangerous. This is troubling when one considers that approximately 80% of 13-18-year-olds now have their own smartphones, and most are daily users of social media applications (Rideout & Robb, 2018). Instagram has been rated as the most important social media network among adolescents (Guimaraes, 2014), and recent evidence indicates that some adolescents may have limited critical thinking skills when assessing online information, especially in a social media environment like Twitter (Breakstone et al., In Press;McGrew et al., 2017McGrew et al., , 2018)).

The time series plot (Figure 1) indicates that there was an emotional response to policy changes regarding self-harm on social media. Further, our topic modeling reveals that most high-level topics on the platform consist of some form of article/blog headline. While a large peak in response to the banning of graphic imagery on Instagram and Facebook was expected, there were unexpected peaks due to the Children's survey, media portrayals of self-harm, exemplars of celebrities and non-celebrities who self-harm, and the use of selfharm as a symbolic term to describe political discourse (i.e., Brexit). The impact of media portrayals of self-harm is evident by the high number of tweets on specific events like the MOMO challenge. MOMO is essentially an online chain letter that challenged children and adolescents to commit self-harm and suicide, along with encouragement to post grotesque images. Peak events appeared to range from these social media scares and celebrity selfharm events to more policy-driven topics like the narratives of Instagram and Facebook leaders and their subsequent interactions with health officials and politicians regarding the ban of graphic self-harm imagery.

The sentiment analysis documented the change in emotional content with a decrease in disgust, joy, surprise, and trust following the ban as well as an increase in anger, anticipation, and sadness. This supports the notion that policy changes related to graphic self-harm content online evoked the emotionality of social media users involved in the discussion. The topic models highlight the anticipation of Twitter users to the new policy and their sensitivity towards images of self-harm and suicidality. Moreover, before the ban, tweet responses demonstrated user awareness of the behaviors of social media leaders, health politicians, and they were influenced by exemplars of self-harm. This suggests that users respond to both the policy aspects that were impending, as well as individual' expressions of self-harm by social media influencers. Following the ban, the topic models show that users evaluated the impact of the ban on graphic self-harm imagery coupled with a continued focus on individual stories of self-harm and media portrayals of self-harm in television shows, celebrities, and social media scares.

Of note, the headquarters of social media platforms (Instagram, Facebook, Twitter) and the celebrity exemplars of self-harm (i.e., Jada Pinkett Smith) are based in the United States. In contrast, the impetus for policy change that includes noncelebrity exemplars (Molly Russell and her father), interactions with health officials and politicians (Health Minister, Ministry of Justice), and academic surveys of self-harm are entirely UK based. This geographical difference requires additional research to explain.

The link between media and suicide has been documented (Arendt, 2018). Previous studies of suicide and online dialogue suggest that Twitter could serve as an effective method for tracking disease and mental illness, perhaps delivering interventions faster than more traditional methods of health care (Jashinsky et al., 2014). In lieu of psychometric scales, there has been creation of risk response classification systems and health care responses that rely on the distinct linguistic profiles of suicide-related tweets (O'Dea et al., 2015). The current study indicates that assessments of emotional discourse around self-harm policy on social media may offer efficacy in designing and redesigning social media platforms with specific users in mind. Specifically, a ban of all graphic images of selfharm might leave some self-harmers without a support system they have leaned on for staying clean and celebrating important milestones without self-harming. Further, adolescents obtain health information online and may prefer to receive help for self-harm desistance via social media platforms (Lewis et al., 2012). Within, we find high engagement with individual exemplars; as such, it may be fruitful to employ "stories" for communicating important health information or intervention messages.

The use of a large data set, including sentiment analysis and topic modeling to capture emotional discourse, demonstrates that tweets were influenced by a policy change regarding graphic imagery of self-harm, as well as the narratives of exemplars of self-harm on social media.

The recognition of the role of social media on self-harm lends justification to the Pediatrics Family Media Use Plan and boundary guidelines for using online resources (Moreno et al., 2016). This may also include the introduction of apps that alert friends and family to impending acts of self-harm (Jashinsky et al., 2014). The analyses within suggest a redesign of self-harm interventions on social media can be improved based on the response to the policy ban. We see that discourse is driven by individual stories (e.g., Jada Pinkett Smith and Molly Russell). This suggests that interventions that use personal stories, particularly those featuring celebrities, may be worth investigating as an addition to the standard sensitivity screen and pro-health messages. These findings support the notion that narratives could lead to increased engagement with the intervention and better outcomes overall.

Poor health information posted on social media can also be ameliorated by educating companies on the appropriate reporting and depiction of self-injury images. Westers et al. (2020) have developed research-informed, consensus-based guidelines for media professionals that maximize positive outcomes (i.e., highlight efforts to seek treatment, stories of recovery, adaptive coping strategies) while minimizing negative outcomes (i.e., avoiding stigmatizing language, removal of description of cutting tools and techniques). This approach specifically focuses on self-injury and social media, including the responsible moderation of platforms where users are aware of rules and face consequences for infractions (Westers et al., 2020).

There are also strategies that focus on encouraging adolescents (and their families) to use social media responsibly. That is, there is potential for "digital hygiene" interventions that temporarily remove youth from all online activities or minimize the negative effects while amplifying benefits (Weinstein et al., 2021). As Weinstein et al. (2021) states, "such interventions might focus on (for example) teaching strategies to regulate digital habits, unfollowing/muting accounts that routinely evoke comparison, and encouraging online engagement with adaptive interests/hobbies and supportive social ties" (p. 71).

Due to the large number of postings, Westers et al. (2020) suggest that the "development of algorithmic or machine learning methods for enforcing platform guidelines is a promising approach for mitigating risk to vulnerable populations, such as those who self-injure" (p. 2). There is a lot of work aimed at improving methods for doing this; for example, Alhassan and Pennington (2020), Alhassan et al. (2021), andWang et al., (2017). Gillespie (2020) adds that the immense scale of data, the relentlessness of the violations, and the need for human judgements lends itself to the use of automated content moderation via artificial intelligence. This could also include the use of the "Platform Governance Observatory," a set of tools that continuously collect public social media content and identify content that has been removed (see Suzor, 2020).

Artificial intelligence has demonstrated utility in assessing risk of suicide for vulnerable populations such as LGBTQ teens and military veterans (Ohlheiser & Hao, 2021), and mobile technologies are developing for "Just-in-Time Adaptive" interventions for suicide prevention and response (Coppersmith et al., 2021). "Just in time" interventions measure fluctuations in suicidal thinking in real-time and can alert health care providers when rapid changes indicating suicidal behavior occurs. Technological developments and novel digital formats allow increased access to suicide prevention (see Kazdin, 2019;Kazdin & Blasé, 2011), though more work is needed to extend these tools to addressed self-injurious behaviors as expressed on social media platforms.

While artificial intelligence has been successful in many avenues of assessing risk and identifying vulnerable users, it is not always perfectly accurate or designed. Based on the reaction to the ban, we see promise in the use of "mute words," something currently available on Twitter, where users have an option to specify mute words that could be potentially harmful to them. We see this approach as particularly beneficial as it appears that an outright ban of graphic images of self-harm not pe rfectly implemented, as it left some users feeling like they're bodies are censored, and they've lost an important support system. We hope that future work will explore the use of customizable screening based on some defaults that can be altered via user-specification.

While a large peak in response to the banning of graphic imagery on Instagram and Facebook was expected, there were unexpected peaks due to the Children's survey, media portrayals of self-harm, exemplars of celebrities and non-celebrities who self-harm, and the use of selfharm as a symbolic term to describe political discourse (i.e., Brexit). The impact of media portrayals of self-harm is evident by the high number of tweets on specific events like the MOMO challenge. MOMO is essentially an online chain letter that challenged children and adolescents to commit self-harm and suicide, along with encouragement to post grotesque images. Peak events appeared to range from these social media scares and celebrity selfharm events to more policy-driven topics like the narratives of Instagram and Facebook leaders and their subsequent interactions with health officials and politicians regarding the ban of graphic self-harm imagery.

The sentiment analysis documented the change in emotional content with a decrease in disgust, joy, surprise, and trust following the ban as well as an increase in anger, anticipation, and sadness. This supports the notion that policy changes related to graphic self-harm content online evoked the emotionality of social media users involved in the discussion. The topic models highlight the anticipation of Twitter users to the new policy and their sensitivity towards images of self-harm and suicidality. Moreover, before the ban, tweet responses demonstrated user awareness of the behaviors of social media leaders, health politicians, and they were influenced by exemplars of self-harm. This suggests that users respond to both the policy aspects that were impending, as well as individual' expressions of self-harm by social media influencers. Following the ban, the topic models show that users evaluated the impact of the ban on graphic self-harm imagery coupled with a continued focus on individual stories of self-harm and media portrayals of self-harm in television shows, celebrities, and social media scares.

Of note, the headquarters of social media platforms (Instagram, Facebook, Twitter) and the celebrity exemplars of self-harm (i.e., Jada Pinkett Smith) are based in the United States. In contrast, the impetus for policy change that includes noncelebrity exemplars (Molly Russell and her father), interactions with health officials and politicians (Health Minister, Ministry of Justice), and academic surveys of self-harm are entirely UK based. This geographical difference requires additional research to explain.

The link between media and suicide has been documented (Arendt, 2018). Previous studies of suicide and online dialogue suggest that Twitter could serve as an effective method for tracking disease and mental illness, perhaps delivering interventions faster than more traditional methods of health care (Jashinsky et al., 2014). In lieu of psychometric scales, there has been creation of risk response classification systems and health care responses that rely on the distinct linguistic profiles of suicide-related tweets (O'Dea et al., 2015). The current study indicates that assessments of emotional discourse around self-harm policy on social media may offer efficacy in designing and redesigning social media platforms with specific users in mind. Specifically, a ban of all graphic images of selfharm might leave some self-harmers without a support system they have leaned on for staying clean and celebrating important milestones without self-harming. Further, adolescents obtain health information online and may prefer to receive help for self-harm desistance via social media platforms (Lewis et al., 2012). Within, we find high engagement with individual exemplars; as such, it may be fruitful to employ "stories" for communicating important health information or intervention messages.

The use of a large data set, including sentiment analysis and topic modeling to capture emotional discourse, demonstrates that tweets were influenced by a policy change regarding graphic imagery of self-harm, as well as the narratives of exemplars of self-harm on social media.

The recognition of the role of social media on self-harm lends justification to the Pediatrics Family Media Use Plan and boundary guidelines for using online resources (Moreno et al., 2016). This may also include the introduction of apps that alert friends and family to impending acts of self-harm (Jashinsky et al., 2014). The analyses within suggest a redesign of self-harm interventions on social media can be improved based on the response to the policy ban. We see that discourse is driven by individual stories (e.g., Jada Pinkett Smith and Molly Russell). This suggests that interventions that use personal stories, particularly those featuring celebrities, may be worth investigating as an addition to the standard sensitivity screen and pro-health messages. These findings support the notion that narratives could lead to increased engagement with the intervention and better outcomes overall.

Poor health information posted on social media can also be ameliorated by educating companies on the appropriate reporting and depiction of self-injury images. Westers et al. (2020) have developed research-informed, consensus-based guidelines for media professionals that maximize positive outcomes (i.e., highlight efforts to seek treatment, stories of recovery, adaptive coping strategies) while minimizing negative outcomes (i.e., avoiding stigmatizing language, removal of description of cutting tools and techniques). This approach specifically focuses on self-injury and social media, including the responsible moderation of platforms where users are aware of rules and face consequences for infractions (Westers et al., 2020).

There are also strategies that focus on encouraging adolescents (and their families) to use social media responsibly. That is, there is potential for "digital hygiene" interventions that temporarily remove youth from all online activities or minimize the negative effects while amplifying benefits (Weinstein et al., 2021). As Weinstein et al. (2021) states, "such interventions might focus on (for example) teaching strategies to regulate digital habits, unfollowing/muting accounts that routinely evoke comparison, and encouraging online engagement with adaptive interests/hobbies and supportive social ties" (p. 71).

Due to the large number of postings, Westers et al. (2020) suggest that the "development of algorithmic or machine learning methods for enforcing platform guidelines is a promising approach for mitigating risk to vulnerable populations, such as those who self-injure" (p. 2). There is a lot of work aimed at improving methods for doing this; for example, Alhassan and Pennington (2020), Alhassan et al. (2021), andWang et al., (2017). Gillespie (2020) adds that the immense scale of data, the relentlessness of the violations, and the need for human judgements lends itself to the use of automated content moderation via artificial intelligence. This could also include the use of the "Platform Governance Observatory," a set of tools that continuously collect public social media content and identify content that has been removed (see Suzor, 2020).

Artificial intelligence has demonstrated utility in assessing risk of suicide for vulnerable populations such as LGBTQ teens and military veterans (Ohlheiser & Hao, 2021), and mobile technologies are developing for "Just-in-Time Adaptive" interventions for suicide prevention and response (Coppersmith et al., 2021). "Just in time" interventions measure fluctuations in suicidal thinking in real-time and can alert health care providers when rapid changes indicating suicidal behavior occurs. Technological developments and novel digital formats allow increased access to suicide prevention (see Kazdin, 2019;Kazdin & Blasé, 2011), though more work is needed to extend these tools to addressed self-injurious behaviors as expressed on social media platforms.

While artificial intelligence has been successful in many avenues of assessing risk and identifying vulnerable users, it is not always perfectly accurate or designed. Based on the reaction to the ban, we see promise in the use of "mute words," something currently available on Twitter, where users have an option to specify mute words that could be potentially harmful to them. We see this approach as particularly beneficial as it appears that an outright ban of graphic images of self-harm not pe rfectly implemented, as it left some users feeling like they're bodies are censored, and they've lost an important support system. We hope that future work will explore the use of customizable screening based on some defaults that can be altered via user-specification.

sections.paper_id: Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: The strength of this study is the use of a large dataset and data mining techniques, such as sentiment analysis and topic modeling, to investigate tweets before and after the Instagram and Facebook ban of graphic self-harm images. A limitation is the lack of demographic data, which is a common challenge for research on adolescent self-harm and the internet. While adolescence carries higher risk of self-harm and young people seek health information on the internet, no identifying information or individual data is included. Additionally, the NRC includes a simple lexicon, a dictionary of words and sentiment for over 14,000 words. We note, further, that a general-use sentiment dictionary may not be specific enough to capture all the unique sentiments and emotions connected to self-harm, and developing a subjectspecific dictionary for self-harm could be of interest to methodological researchers. Although constructing sentiment using the NRC lexicon has several shortfalls compared to human readers, for example, words are scored in isolation, thus the effect of modifiers are ignored, this lexicon allows researchers to evaluate a large corpus of tweets, automating sentiment analysis to see what emotion content is present. For a comprehensive overview of several sentiment analysis methods and a thorough discussion about the performance issues when compared to human coding see van Atteveldt et al. (2021). Lastly, topic model analysis approaches employing LDA have seen tremendous success, yielding thousands of applications displaying its ability to effectively classify documents in a variety of settings, but have similar drawbacks and rely on human validation which injects some subjectivity to the interpretation of the analyses. To ameliorate this as much as possible, we've tried to tie topics to real events wherever possible and generously cite sources to corroborate the interpretation externally.

sections.paper_id: Smith and Cipolli - 2022 - The InstagramFacebook ban on graphic self‐harm imagery A sentiment analysis and topic modeling app
paragraphs: To inform and protect vulnerable adolescents from misleading health messages regarding self-harm, it is important to assess policy changes that impact emotions on social media platforms. Findings from this study indicate that a ban of graphic imagery of self-harm produced peaks in activity over time, with changes in emotional expressions regarding the new policy. Furthermore, reactions to the ban included topics were influenced by noncelebrity exemplars of self-harm, celebrity narratives, and social media scares, as well as the statements made by social media leaders and their interactions with health organization and politicians.

We hope that this study helps motivate future ethnographic work that more deeply explores the topics we see at the surface, understanding that this does not paint the full picture for those struggling with self-harm and suicidal thoughts. Specifically, we hope this analysis helps kindle new questions about and deeper research into the online experiences of those struggling with self-harm and suicide. Specifically, there is a lot of room for further exploration about how social media can be designed and redesigned to help support those that need it in the ways that they need it.

While those making decisions have decided removing all self-harm content to be the best approach, that policy removes/censors the support channels for some who use Twitter for social support and celebrating recovery. Investigating the reaction to policy further can allow the design and redesign conversation about online to be re-centered around those susceptible to, struggling with, or recovering from self-harm.
